---
title: "Is the model's complete DTS Ca²⁺ depletion a real discrepancy? — a literature check"
author: "Steve Haigh (with Claude Code)"
date: "2026-06-14"
format:
  pdf:
    include-in-header: ../pandoc-header.tex
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
---

# TL;DR — the verdict

**Partly a real discrepancy, and regime-specific.** Our model drains the dense
tubular system (DTS) to ~0 µM under the standard *saturating, sustained* agonist;
Dolan & Diamond 2014 retain ~120–180 µM. The literature is genuinely *split* on
whether a store empties completely under maximal IP₃, but the weight of evidence
— and Dolan's own setup — says real stores **retain a substantial fraction
(~30–50%)** because IP₃ receptors *inactivate* before the store empties. So our
near-complete depletion sits at the aggressive end and most likely **over-depletes**.

Three things make this not a crisis, and point to what to do:

1. **It is regime-dependent.** The complete depletion is specific to the
   saturating-sustained regime. We already saw the model's store *partially*
   refill (to ~66 µM) under a transient/threshold stimulus — i.e. it behaves
   Dolan-like when stimulated Dolan-style.
2. **It doesn't break the validated result.** Complete depletion *maximises* SOCE,
   which is exactly the SOCE differential the Dolan 5/5 criteria reward. This is a
   recovery-phase *fidelity* issue, not a core failure.
3. **The mechanistic cause is identifiable** (see §5): our IP₃R's cytosolic-Ca²⁺
   inactivation is too weak to close the channel at the plateau, and we model no
   store-retention mechanism — so the (Nernst) channel equilibrates the store to
   ~0 whenever IP₃ is held high.

**Recommendation:** make this a *dose-response* result rather than a calibration
fight — characterise store depletion vs agonist dose, show it deepens from
partial (threshold) to near-complete (saturating), and check the intermediate
doses land in Dolan's band. Treat a stronger IP₃R-inactivation / store-retention
term as an optional, *defensible-range* modelling choice, not a forced fix.

---

# 1. What the model does, and why (recap)

Under the default saturating agonist (1 nM thrombin + 10 µM ADP + 10 µM ATP):

- IP₃ rises and **stays elevated** (~220 nM) because the irreversibly-cleaved
  PAR1 and the v0.61 autocrine loops (TXA₂→TP→Gq, secreted ADP→P2Y1) sustain Gq.
- The IP₃R is modelled as a **Nernst-driven channel** (de Young–Keizer / Li–Rinzel
  open probability $\times$ a GHK driving force). Whenever it is appreciably open
  it tends to *equilibrate* the two compartments, so it cannot hold a DTS↔cytosol
  gradient.
- Its only closing mechanism is cytosolic-Ca²⁺ inactivation (the slow `h` gate).
  At our plateau (cyt ≈ 430 nM) that inactivation is only partial
  ($h_\infty \approx 0.7$ for a ~1 µM inactivation constant), so the channel
  stays substantially open → the store drains to ~cytosolic levels (~0 µM on the
  µM scale) and SOCE pins wide open.

There is **no luminal-Ca²⁺ regulation of the IP₃R and no other store-retention
term** in the model.

# 2. What Dolan actually assumes (from the 2014 supplement)

This is the crux of why Dolan retains 120–180 µM and we don't. Dolan's IP₃ is an
explicit **transient**: from the supporting material, *"IP₃ rises to about 5-fold
higher than its resting value within 5 s of stimulation, and returns to a new
steady level within 200 s."* A transient IP₃ lets the IP₃R *close* in the
recovery phase, so SERCA partially refills the store → partial depletion.

Two further points from Dolan's text:

- Dolan's "best" model configurations deliberately favour a **high IP₃R/SERCA
  ratio** and *slow* refilling so that SOCE contributes meaningfully to cytosolic
  Ca²⁺ — i.e. Dolan *wants* substantial depletion, just not total.
- The 120–180 µM `dts_min` is a **model output** (Fig. 4A) of an *estimate*, not a
  direct measurement (see §4 on why direct platelet measurements are scarce).

So a large part of the difference is **stimulus protocol** (his transient IP₃ vs
our sustained drive), not a different store model — exactly the framing of the
recovery-phase work this session.

# 3. The general ER picture: does a store empty completely?

The literature is **split**, and the split is mechanistically informative.

**View A — partial release / quantal, IP₃Rs inactivate (store stays ~half full).**
Mataragka & Taylor 2021 (*Cell Reports*; "Quantal Ca²⁺ release mediated by very
few IP₃ receptors that rapidly inactivate allows graded responses to IP₃",
PMID 34731613) found that **even a *maximal* agonist concentration released only
52% ± 3% of the stores** — i.e. **~48% stays trapped** — because IP₃Rs activate
then *rapidly inactivate*; "only a tiny fraction of a cell's IP₃Rs mediate
incremental Ca²⁺ release." Notably they concluded **luminal Ca²⁺ is *not* the
mechanism** of quantal release in their system — it is kinetic IP₃R inactivation.

**View B — sustained maximal IP₃ keeps the store nearly empty.** The opposing
argument (e.g. discussion around IP₃R–STIM1 coupling, Nat. Sci. Rep. 2018,
s41598-018-31621-0): because a single open IP₃R conducts ~5×10⁵ Ca²⁺·s⁻¹ while a
SERCA pumps <40 Ca²⁺·s⁻¹, *"very little residual IP₃R activity"* can overwhelm
SERCA and **keep the ER depleted** under maintained stimulation. IP₃R–STIM1
association is even reported to create a *reduced intraluminal Ca²⁺
microenvironment* to enhance SOCE.

**Reconciliation.** The two are not contradictory once you separate *transient/
graded* from *sustained-maximal* stimulation: graded IP₃ → quantal, partial
emptying (View A); strongly sustained IP₃ → near-complete emptying (View B). Our
model lives in View B by construction (sustained IP₃, weak inactivation); Dolan's
transient lives nearer View A. **The empirically supported "default", however, is
that physiological agonist stimulation leaves a substantial residual store** —
because in real cells IP₃Rs inactivate harder/faster than ours do.

Resting ER free [Ca²⁺] is ~few hundred µM (≈300–700 µM; ER-depletion review,
PMC3098671), consistent with our ~250 µM resting DTS.

# 4. Platelet-specific evidence (sparse — handle with care)

Direct measurement of **free luminal [Ca²⁺] in the platelet DTS is genuinely
hard** (platelets are tiny, anucleate, and the DTS is a thin reticulum), so most
platelet store work reports *Ca content* / Mn²⁺-quench / thapsigargin-releasable
pools rather than free luminal concentration. Key qualitative points:

- Platelets have (at least) **two functional Ca²⁺ stores** — the DTS (SERCA3-rich,
  TBHQ/thapsigargin-sensitive) and **acidic/lysosome-like granules** (SERCA2b);
  thrombin releases from both (PMC1698687). Our model lumps only the DTS, so a
  single "store" already simplifies the biology.
- The DTS undergoes **rapid ultrastructural collapse** (thin→vesicular) within
  ~10 s of thrombin (*Blood* 1992, 80:718) — consistent with strong, fast
  depletion, but ultrastructure is not a free-[Ca²⁺] readout.
- **"Supramaximal" Ca²⁺ signals** drive procoagulant (phosphatidylserine⁺)
  platelet formation (*Blood Advances* 2020, 4:154) — i.e. the strongest
  physiological responses involve very deep, sustained Ca²⁺ mobilisation. So a
  near-empty DTS is *not* unphysiological at the extreme end of activation.
- **Acidic-store depletion is required for aggregation** (PMID 19587585) —
  reinforcing that deep store depletion accompanies full activation.

Net: platelet data are consistent with *deep* depletion under strong thrombin but
do **not** pin a free-[Ca²⁺] residual, and there is no platelet measurement that
clearly says "the DTS goes to zero" or "it holds 150 µM." Dolan's band is the
best available *estimate*, not ground truth.

## Does the proteome (Burkhart 2012) help? — abundance vs kinetics

Burkhart 2012 (*Blood* 120:e73, the platelet proteome the model leans on) is a
*parts list* — copy numbers, not kinetics — so it constrains the store
*machinery's capacity* but is the wrong instrument for the depletion-*extent*
question. Two concrete things come out of it, and both **reinforce the verdict**.

**1. The abundance ratio points to *deeper*, not shallower, depletion — so the
lever must be kinetics.** Burkhart's store-machinery counts (per reviews quoting
its supplemental Table 3; *verify against the primary table before thesis use*):

| protein | Burkhart 2012 | model (via Dolan Table S1) |
|---|---|---|
| IP₃R (ITPR1 / 2 / 3) | ~2,400 / 1,700 / 750 → **~4,850** | **1,328** |
| SERCA (2 + 3) | ~9,000 / 16,300 → **~25,300** | **11,892** |
| STIM1 | **~33,000** | ~4,265 |
| Orai1 | ~1,700 | ~1,447 |

The IP₃R:SERCA *ratio* is ~1:5 in Burkhart vs ~1:9 in the model. By Dolan's own
logic (higher IP₃R/SERCA → deeper depletion, slower refill), Burkhart's ratio
would, if anything, drive the store *more* completely empty, not less. So **the
proteome does not rescue partial depletion** — confirming that the residual-store
behaviour seen in real cells is an **IP₃R-inactivation (kinetic)** property, which
a proteome cannot see, not an abundance one.

**2. Useful side-findings + citation flags.**

- **ITPR1 is the most abundant platelet IP₃R isoform** (~2,400), not ITPR2 — so
  the model's generic de Young–Keizer (ITPR1-type, biphasic-Ca) gating is a
  defensible isoform choice.
- The model's store counts trace to **Dolan Table S1, not Burkhart**, and they
  differ materially (IP₃R ~3.6×, SERCA ~2×, STIM1 ~8× higher in Burkhart). The
  **STIM1 8× gap** is the most striking (Burkhart ~33,000 vs model ~4,265); STIM1
  is the depletion *sensor*, so it is worth reconciling on the SOCE side. The
  code comment attributing `N_IP3R = 1328` to "Burkhart 2012" is inaccurate —
  that is Dolan's number; Burkhart's total IP₃R is ~4,850.
- **No direct free-[Ca²⁺]_DTS measurement in platelets** turned up — the field
  measures Ca *content* / releasable pools, not free luminal concentration
  (Mag-Fura/Mag-Fluo ER methods exist generally; ER free [Ca²⁺] ~100 µM–1 mM,
  consistent with the ~250 µM resting DTS). This confirms Dolan's band is an
  *estimate*, and is itself a genuine gap in the field — not something more
  searching will fill.

# 5. Verdict and mechanistic root

**Is complete depletion a real discrepancy? Yes, mildly — and specifically in the
saturating-sustained regime.** Three defensible statements:

1. Near-complete depletion under a *saturating, sustained* agonist is *not*
   physically impossible (View B), and the procoagulant/"supramaximal" literature
   shows the extreme of platelet activation does deplete very deeply.
2. But the better-supported expectation for *ordinary* agonist stimulation is a
   **substantial residual store (~30–50%)**, because IP₃Rs inactivate (View A,
   quantal release) — and Dolan's 120–180 µM is consistent with that. Our ~0 µM
   therefore **over-depletes** for the typical case.
3. The cause is specific and fixable: **our IP₃R inactivation is too weak to close
   the channel at the plateau, and we model no store-retention mechanism.** With a
   Nernst flux and a channel that stays ~30% available at cyt ≈ 430 nM, sustained
   IP₃ inevitably equilibrates the store to ~0.
4. **The proteome backs this up.** Burkhart's IP₃R:SERCA abundance ratio (~1:5) is
   *higher* than the model's (~1:9), so by Dolan's logic it favours *deeper*
   depletion — the residual-store behaviour is a kinetics (inactivation) property,
   not an abundance one (see the Burkhart subsection in §4).

# 6. What it would take to move the model (if we choose to)

Options, in increasing intervention:

- **(a) Reframe, don't change** — present complete depletion as the saturating-
  sustained limit, validated by the dose-response showing partial depletion at
  lower doses (this is the cheapest, and it's already half-done by the
  recovery-phase work).
- **(b) Strengthen IP₃R inactivation** — make the `h`-gate (de Young–Keizer
  inactivation constant / rate) close the channel harder at elevated cyt Ca²⁺, so
  that even under sustained IP₃ a residual store survives. This is the
  most literature-aligned lever (quantal release = strong kinetic inactivation).
  Caveat: it touches the core Ca²⁺ ODE → regenerate goldens, re-verify Dolan 5/5,
  and check it doesn't blunt the peak.
- **(c) Add a store-retention mechanism** — a luminal-Ca²⁺-dependent IP₃R term or
  an explicit lumenal buffer floor. More biology, more parameters; note that the
  *quantal-release* work argues luminal Ca²⁺ is **not** the mechanism, so (b) is
  better motivated than (c).

All of (b)/(c) are **defensible-range modelling choices, not a forced fix** — the
literature does not mandate a single residual value, so this is calibration with
honest uncertainty, not error-correction.

# 7. Recommendation

1. **Make it a dose-response result.** Sweep agonist dose and record DTS minimum
   vs dose. Predicted/expected shape: partial depletion at threshold deepening to
   near-complete at saturation. If intermediate doses land in Dolan's 120–180 µM
   band, that simultaneously (i) validates the store dynamics across regimes and
   (ii) recasts the complete depletion as the saturating limit, not a defect.
   Pair with the integrin PAC-1 and secretion readouts already in place — this is
   the natural next chapter and ties the v0.61 mechanisms together.
2. **Keep complete depletion for now**; only adopt option (b) if a reviewer/the
   thesis narrative needs the *typical*-stimulus store to hold a residual. If so,
   strengthen IP₃R inactivation (option b), regenerate goldens, re-verify Dolan
   5/5, and snapshot a before/after store-depletion figure.
3. **Flag the measurement-uncertainty caveat** in the thesis: free luminal DTS
   [Ca²⁺] is not directly measured in platelets, so Dolan's band is an estimate —
   don't over-fit to 120–180 µM.

---

# References

*Citation hygiene: verify every journal/volume/DOI in Zotero before thesis use
(only the [VERIFIED] item below was confirmed against the library this session).*

- **[VERIFIED]** Dolan AT, Diamond SL (2014). *Systems Modeling of Ca²⁺
  Homeostasis and Mobilization in Platelets Mediated by IP₃ and Store-Operated
  Ca²⁺ Entry.* Biophysical Journal 106(9):2049–2060. DOI
  10.1016/j.bpj.2014.03.028. (Confirmed via Zotero, incl. supporting material:
  transient IP₃ forcing, IP₃R/SERCA-ratio discussion, `dts_min` as a model
  output.)
- **[VERIFIED]** Burkhart JM, Vaudel M, Gambaryan S, et al. (2012). *The first
  comprehensive and quantitative analysis of human platelet protein composition…*
  Blood 120(15):e73–82. DOI 10.1182/blood-2012-04-416594. (Confirmed via Zotero.
  Main-text Table 1 gives αIIb ~83,300 / β3 ~64,200 / gelsolin ~52,900 / PKCβ
  ~9,700. **The store-machinery counts — IP₃R ~4,850, SERCA ~25,300, STIM1
  ~33,000, Orai1 ~1,700 — are quoted here via secondary reviews of Burkhart's
  supplemental Table 3 and must be checked against the primary supplement before
  thesis use.**)
- Purvis JE, Chatterjee MS, Brass LF, Diamond SL (2008). *A molecular signaling
  model of platelet phosphoinositide and calcium regulation during homeostasis
  and P2Y1 activation.* Blood 112:4069–4079. (Already in the model; Dolan's IP₃
  shape is "loosely based" on this.)
- Mataragka S?, Taylor CW (2021). *Quantal Ca²⁺ release mediated by very few IP₃
  receptors that rapidly inactivate allows graded responses to IP₃.* Cell Reports.
  PMID 34731613. — **~52% released at maximal agonist (~48% retained); IP₃R rapid
  inactivation; luminal Ca²⁺ ruled out as the quantal mechanism.** *(First author
  to confirm.)*
- *Endoplasmic-Reticulum Calcium Depletion and Disease.* Review, PMC3098671. —
  resting ER free [Ca²⁺] ~hundreds of µM; SERCA-uptake/leak balance.
- *Reliable measurement of free Ca²⁺ concentrations in the ER lumen using
  Mag-Fluo-4.* PMC7181174. — low-affinity ER indicators; ER free [Ca²⁺]
  ~100 µM–1 mM (method context; no platelet-specific value).
- *Association of the IP₃R to STIM1 provides a reduced intraluminal calcium
  microenvironment…* Sci. Rep. 2018, s41598-018-31621-0. — residual-IP₃R /
  SERCA-conductance argument for sustained depletion; IP₃R–STIM1 coupling.
- *Differential involvement of thrombin receptors in Ca²⁺ release from two
  different intracellular stores in human platelets.* PMC1698687. — DTS +
  acidic/lysosomal stores.
- *Rapid ultrastructural changes in the dense tubular system following platelet
  activation.* Blood 1992, 80(3):718. — DTS thin→vesicular within ~10 s of
  thrombin.
- *Supramaximal calcium signaling triggers procoagulant platelet formation.*
  Blood Advances 2020, 4(1):154. — deepest activation = very deep, sustained Ca²⁺.
- *Acidic-store depletion is required for human platelet aggregation.* PMID
  19587585.

*Render: `quarto render reports/design/dts-depletion-literature-2026-06-14.qmd`
(or `make quarto-pdfs`).*
